findings.justification: When any ancestor of the requested path is a symlink, the code falls back to $originalFullPath (the unresolved, user-supplied path) instead of a realpath-validated path, allowing an attacker with a symlinked directory to escape the designated image root.

MIME type validation relies solely on file extension via wp_check_filetype / get_allowed_mime_types without content-based inspection, and the symlink-escape bypass can redirect the upload destination outside the web root, enabling potentially dangerous file placement.
Raw $error_code values are reflected into the error notification message without sanitization, leaking internal server-side PHP/upload error codes to the end user.

The $error_code variable is concatenated directly into the string passed to Notification::error() without HTML-escaping; if that notification is rendered in an HTML context without escaping, it could allow script injection.
